question: A 60-year-old man presented with a painful, enlarging anterior chest wall swelling. Biopsy demonstrated a grade II primary bone tumor and he received three cycles of chemotherapy without response. Based on the imaging and clinical course, which surgical approach is most appropriate?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image of the upper anterior chest. There is a large, lobulated destructive mass centered on the manubrium/upper sternum with cortical destruction of the bone, internal punctate calcified densities, and a bulky soft-tissue component that extends both anteriorly into the subcutaneous/pectoral region and posteriorly towards the mediastinum, abutting mediastinal structures and vascular contours.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical stem. The patient is a 60-year-old man with a painful, enlarging anterior chest wall mass, biopsy-proven grade II primary bone tumor, and he has already had three cycles of chemotherapy without response. The tumor’s size, bone destruction, and clear intrathoracic extension on imaging indicate a multi-compartment lesion that has not regressed with systemic therapy.

Then, I consider how each surgical option lines up with those observations. Limited curettage and simple closure would leave gross residual disease because the lesion is destructive and extends beyond the cortex into adjacent soft tissues and the mediastinum. “Resection sparing intrathoracic component” would similarly leave tumor in the intrathoracic compartment seen on the CT. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation first is unlikely to be beneficial given the documented lack of response to three cycles of chemotherapy and the pressing need to remove bulky, locally destructive disease. Palliative debulking without reconstruction would not address the need for a definitive oncologic resection or restore chest-wall stability after removal of this extensive lesion.

Therefore, the imaging and clinical course support a single-stage oncologic approach that removes the entire involved bone and contiguous soft tissue (an en bloc resection) with planned chest-wall reconstruction to restore structural integrity and protect intrathoracic contents.
caption: Pre-operative computed tomography (CT) of anterior chest wall chondrosarcoma arising from the sternum with internal calcification
sub_caption: Pre-operative computed tomography (CT) of anterior chest wall chondrosarcoma arising from the sternum with internal calcification
image_context: A 60-year-old male patient, known to have hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and a history of cardiac catheterization and cholecystectomy, was referred to the hospital with painful enlarging swelling of the anterior chest. The patient also developed at that time progressive shortness of breath and a productive cough. He denied any history of relevant trauma to the area. The physical examination revealed a hard anterior chest mass measuring 8 × 8 × 8 cm. The abdomen was soft and lax with no palpable organomegaly. Chest computed tomography (CT) showed an extensive, destructive lesion involving the whole manubrium with a soft tissue component of 8 × 8 × 6 cm. The destructive osseous lesion with internal calcification in the sternum was associated with an anterior parasternal soft tissue component and another posterior intrathoracic state intermediate soft tissue component, which was abutting with the left brachiocephalic vein (Fig. <xref rid="13019_2023_2213_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>). There was evidence of a few bilateral non-specific pulmonary lesions. The patient underwent three cycles of chemotherapy without response. Findings raised the possibility of a primary bone tumor without definite pulmonary metastasis. Chondrosarcoma was a top differential. Positron emission tomography PET-CT demonstrated a heterogeneous mildly hypermetabolic (low FDG-avid) large expansive destructive manubrial mass with intrathoracic (superior anterior mediastinal) and extrathoracic soft tissue components. This was compatible with the primary chondrosarcoma. Biopsy showed grade II chondrosarcoma. Therefore, the patient was referred to a neoplasm specialized center, King Hussein Cancer Center.). relevant_image_context: A 60-year-old male patient presented with a painful enlarging swelling of the anterior chest. Chest computed tomography (CT) showed an extensive, destructive lesion involving the whole manubrium with a soft tissue component of 8 × 8 × 6 cm. The destructive osseous lesion with internal calcification in the sternum was associated with an anterior parasternal soft tissue component and another posterior intrathoracic component, which was abutting the left brachiocephalic vein. There was evidence of a few bilateral non-specific pulmonary lesions. The patient underwent three cycles of chemotherapy without response. Findings raised the possibility of a primary bone tumor without definite pulmonary metastasis. Chondrosarcoma was a top differential. Positron emission tomography PET-CT demonstrated a heterogeneous mildly hypermetabolic (low FDG-avid) large expansive destructive manubrial mass with intrathoracic and extrathoracic soft tissue components. Biopsy showed grade II chondrosarcoma.
